title: Open Science in Moldova: A Regulation Worth Reinforcing with a Secondary Publication Right
content:
Blog post by Mariana Harjevschi

The recent adoption of the Regulation on Open Science in the Republic of Moldova marks an important milestone in the modernisation of the national research system and is a positive step for the academic and research community. By requiring open access to publicly funded research, the development of research data management plans, the use of institutional repositories, and adherence to FAIR principles, Moldova continues to align itself with European Union policies and international Open Science standards.

Grounded in the vision of the National Development Strategy “European Moldova 2030”, the country’s overarching reform agenda, this Regulation is based on a clear and powerful principle: knowledge generated with public funding should be freely accessible to the public. Yet whether the framework goes far enough to secure its own ambitions is a question worth asking.

From Principle to Obligation: What the Regulation Requires
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Licence to Chill: Launching the 100 Contracts Study
content:
Blog post by Ana Lazarova

Libraries are essential infrastructures for research, education, culture, and lifelong learning. Every day, academic, public, research, and school libraries connect people with knowledge, preserve cultural heritage, and support innovation. To fulfil these missions, libraries rely on legal rights that allow them to use copyrighted materials in the public interest.

Knowledge Rights 21 is pleased to launch the 100 Contracts Study, a research project examining how licensing agreements affect libraries’ ability to exercise those rights.

Inspired by a 2008 British Library analysis of licensing agreements, this study, conducted between February and August 2023, evaluates 100 contracts between publishers and academic and public libraries across 14 Council of Europe countries. It draws on a dataset of licensing agreements sourced from libraries within the KR21 and IFLA networks, as well as information consortia, and publicly available publisher terms.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Preventing Contract Override: Recommendations for Libraries and Legislators
content:
Blog post by Benjamin White

A Study of 100 Contracts, published by Knowledge Rights 21, shines a light on the critical issue that contracts severely undermine the ability of  libraries to deliver access to knowledge to support research, education and cultural participation.

With the move from physical ownership to digital licensing, what libraries and users can do is now largely defined by the terms of contracts negotiated with rightsholders.

One major issue for libraries is that there often is not the resource to negotiate every licence. 

Even where resources are available, agreements frequently constitute contracts of adhesion, in which libraries have little, if any, scope to negotiate the contract in any meaningful sense. 

As a result, contractual arrangements can restrict or prohibit certain uses of content that are otherwise allowed by copyright law. 

The override of permitted uses in this way severely threatens and disrupts a library’s work by diluting or removing long-held legally established rights to copy and share content.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: The Digital Omnibus on Data Must Work for Research Too
content:
The Digital Omnibus on Data, the first major piece of legislation to reach the European Parliament and Council in the 2024-2029 mandate, is being built largely around the needs of business.

That leaves two gaps for research and libraries: a definition of ‘research data’ that does not yet cover the metadata needed to find and assess research, and equal protection against unfair contract terms for research institutions seeking to access data. Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) is calling on the European Parliament and Council to close both gaps as the file moves forward.

The Omnibus itself is branded as a simplification exercise, merging the Data Governance Act, the Open Data Directive, and the Data Act, and amending the General Data Protection Regulation, to give data-intensive sectors a single rule-book to follow.

One of the pre-existing pieces of legislation to be absorbed is the Open Data Directive, agreed in 2019 to update the EU’s rules on public sector information.
snippet: The Digital Omnibus on Data, the first major piece of legislation to reach the European Parliament and Council in the 2024-2029 mandate, is being built largely around the needs of business.
creator: Stephen Wyber
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Welcoming the Irish EU Presidency
content:
Throughout the second half of 2026, Irish representatives will take charge of the vast majority of meetings of the European Union’s Member States. As usual, this presidency will be about advancing ongoing legislative agendas, but also offer opportunities to highlight particular national priorities and themes. 

Over the next weeks, there will be lots of presentations of forward plans across different European Parliament committees as well as in different formations of the Council of Ministers. This article highlights those that are particularly relevant in Knowledge Rights 21’s areas of work, and some questions we hope we’ll see answered. 

Maintaining the Legislative Momentum

The Presidency’s overall themes align well with the EU’s wider agenda at the moment, with a focus on competitiveness, values and security. Under the competitiveness pillar, it offers five building blocks – simplification, an integrated single market, championing trade, energy transition and digital and AI. All, again, are close to ongoing priorities.
snippet: Throughout the second half of 2026, Irish representatives will take charge of the vast majority of meetings of the European Union’s Member States. As usual, this presidency will be about advancing

title: Exception to the Exception: A Win for the Netherlands Institute of Sound and Vision
content:
Blog post by Maarten Zeinstra

In November 2025, Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) reported on the systemic challenges facing the Out-of-Commerce (OOC) regime under the EU Copyright in the Digital Single Market (DSM) Directive. KR21, COMMUNIA, and Europeana all argue that significant structural flaws within Articles 8–11 heavily restrict the efficacy of the regime.

However, a recent Dutch success story serves as a notable exception to the slow implementation and systemic bottlenecks seen across Europe. This larger publication of out-of-commerce heritage is the result of a “perfect storm” of policy, funding, and proactive politics. It points to what is possible.

In May 2026, the Netherlands Institute of Sound and Vision (Beeld & Geluid) launched a new collection portal called De Schatkamer (The Treasury). The platform unlocks over 700,000 audio and audiovisual archival items that were previously unavailable on the open internet. These include daily historical newsreels from Dutch public broadcasters and iconic television series—such as Floris starring Rutger Hauer—that are no longer available through customary commercial channels.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Because, Because … ! European Commission Says Consumers Take a Back Seat to Copyright: Erasure of Content Can Continue
content:
Stop Killing Games is just the 14th European Citizens’ Initiative (ECI) in as many years to have received the over 1 million signatures necessary to trigger an obligation for the European Commission to provide a response.

Unfortunately, the response is a limp one, which can be summarised as ‘because because’, or in more words, ‘we can’t change the law because we can’t change the law’. For an institution that is all about making and changing laws, this is curious.

The Commission’s response does, however, increase the pressure to look again at copyright laws themselves in order to ensure that they support the EU’s wider consumer protection goals. In this, the gaming community will find strong allies in libraries, universities, and similar institutions.
snippet: Stop Killing Games is just the 14th European Citizens’ Initiative (ECI) in as many years to have received the over 1 million signatures necessary to trigger an obligation for the European Commission
creator: Stephen Wyber
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Europe’s Copyright Framework Is Failing Researchers
content:
Blog Post by Dr Konrad Gliściński LL.M. (Intellectual Property Law Chair JU/ Centrum Cyfrowe)

Research today is international, digital, data-intensive, and collaborative by default. Scientists routinely work across borders, rely on access to large collections of publications and data, use computational methods such as text and data mining (TDM), and increasingly incorporate AI tools into their research workflows. Yet the legal framework governing access to and use of copyrighted materials for research remains fragmented along national lines.

A new report, Barriers to Research: Insights into the Patchwork of Research Exceptions in European Copyright Laws and Practical Perspectives from European Researchers, prepared by Centrum Cyfrowe in collaboration with the Future Law Lab of the Jagiellonian University, shows that despite decades of copyright harmonisation efforts at the EU level, researchers across Europe still operate within a highly fragmented legal framework, resulting in legal uncertainty and unnecessary obstacles.
snippet: Blog Post by Dr Konrad Gliściński LL.M. (Intellectual Property Law Chair JU/ Centrum Cyfrowe)
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: It Just Ain’t Right: Why Open Access Mandates Without Secondary Publication Rights Are a Recipe for Unfairness
content:
It is looking increasingly likely that a secondary publication right (SPR) will feature on the European Union’s legislative agenda in the coming years. Having been clearly called for by Member States in Council Conclusions in 2023, the European Commission has commissioned two significant pieces of research into SPRs and their impacts. 

Central to the debate ahead is a distinction that often goes unstated: the difference between an obligation to publish open access and a right that makes doing so legally safe for researchers.

Furthermore, it has now called twice for stakeholder inputs, firstly in a consultation led by the Directorate General for Research, Technology and Innovation (DG RTD) in the second half of 2025, and now in one led by the Directorate General for Communications Networks, Content and Technology (DG CNECT), which is open until 25 June 2026.
snippet: It is looking increasingly likely that a secondary publication right (SPR) will feature on the European Union’s legislative agenda in the coming years. Having been clearly called for by Member States
creator: Stephen Wyber

title: Rome Conference Charts the Road Ahead for Open Science under the European Research Area Act
content:
Blog post by Deborah De Angelis

On 10 June 2026, the conference “Open Science through the Prism of the European Research Area (ERA) Act: The Work Still to Be Done” took place at the Central Library of the National Research Council (CNR) in Rome. The event provided an important opportunity for discussion, bringing together policymakers, scholars, legal experts, researchers, institutional representatives, and professionals to address a key issue for the future of European research.

Institutional Voices Set the Tone & Political Commitment for Open Science and SPR
snippet: Blog post by Deborah De Angelis
creator: KR21 Guest
